senses.definition: Large, heavy snakes found mainly in South America that kill prey by wrapping around it and squeezing it.
examples: Anacondas live in swamps and slow rivers in South America.
In the documentary, the camera crew followed several giant anacondas in the wetlands.

senses.definition: Not producing or reflecting sound; completely without echo.
examples: The experiment was carried out in an anacoustic chamber.
In an anacoustic space, even a small noise can feel strange.

senses.definition: An ancient Greek lyric poet known for short poems about love and wine.
examples: Students read Anacreon to understand early Greek lyric poetry.
The poet was sometimes compared to Anacreon for his playful style.

senses.definition: Relating to the poetry of Anacreon, especially light poems about love, drinking, and pleasure.
examples: The poet became famous for his anacreontic verses on wine and music.
They hosted an evening of anacreontic songs and recitations.

senses.definition: A short light poem or song, usually about drinking, love, or simple pleasures, written in the manner of Anacreon.
examples: The choir ended the concert with a cheerful anacreontic.
Eighteenth‑century poets often wrote anacreontics for private gatherings.

senses.definition: In a way that resembles the light, playful poetry of Anacreon, especially about love or drinking.
examples: The poet writes anacreontically about wine and youth.
The lyrics move anacreontically between love and laughter.

senses.definition: Short lyrical poems or verses in the style of Anacreon, usually about love, wine, and enjoyment of life.
examples: Her collection of anacreontics celebrates friendship and wine.
In class we read English anacreontics based on Greek models.

senses.definition: A detailed questioning, especially of a witness, to bring out the truth of a matter.
examples: Through careful anacrisis, the lawyer revealed contradictions in the story.
The report was based on anacrisis of several key witnesses.

senses.definition: A group of liverworts whose female organs develop at the tips of the shoots.
examples: The genus belongs to the anacrogynae within the liverworts.
In anacrogynae, the archegonia are formed at the growing ends of the plant.

senses.definition: Describing liverworts whose female reproductive organs are produced at the tips of the shoots.
examples: The plant has an anacrogynous type of reproductive structure.
These anacrogynous liverworts are common in damp forests.

senses.definition: Relating to a group of birds with a particular arrangement of the muscles of the voice box.
examples: Anacromyodian birds share a similar syrinx structure.
The species was once classified as anacromyodian based on its vocal muscles.

senses.definition: Describing a pulse wave whose rise is interrupted by a small notch or wave on its upward slope.
examples: The doctor noted an anacrotic pulse in the patient’s carotid artery.
An anacrotic wave can indicate certain heart valve problems.

senses.definition: The condition of having an anacrotic pulse wave, where the rise shows a small secondary wave or notch.
examples: Anacrotism may be seen in patients with aortic valve disease.
The tracing clearly demonstrated anacrotism in the arterial waveform.

senses.definition: Unstressed syllables or notes that come before the first strong beat in a line of poetry or a piece of music; upbeats.
examples: The song’s opening anacruses lead smoothly into the first full bar.
In this poem, the anacruses give a feeling of forward movement.

senses.definition: One or more weak syllables or notes at the start of a line of poetry or music, coming before the first strong beat.
examples: The melody begins with a one‑beat anacrusis.
The poet uses an anacrusis to soften the strong opening stress.

senses.definition: Having or beginning with an anacrusis; starting with one or more weak beats or syllables before the main stress.
examples: The piece has an anacrustic opening that leads into a strong downbeat.
In anacrustic lines, the first syllables are not heavily stressed.

senses.definition: In a way that starts with an anacrusis, using weak beats or syllables before the main strong beat.
examples: The melody unfolds anacrustically, creating a sense of anticipation.
The poet writes anacrustically to vary the rhythm of the line.

senses.definition: The growing of organisms, especially microbes or plants, in water that is rich in nutrients.
examples: The lab specialises in anaculture of algae for biofuel research.
Anaculture techniques allow bacteria to be grown in liquid media.

senses.definition: Total loss of the sense of hearing; complete deafness.
examples: After the infection, the patient developed anacusia in one ear.
Severe trauma may lead to permanent anacusia.

senses.definition: Having complete loss of hearing; relating to or affected by anacusia.
examples: An anacusic patient may rely entirely on sign language.
The study focused on anacusic children in primary schools.

senses.definition: Total deafness; complete absence of hearing.
examples: The accident resulted in anacusis in both ears.
Proper ear protection can prevent noise‑induced anacusis.

senses.definition: A genus of plants in the daisy family, including species sometimes used in traditional medicine.
examples: Anacyclus pyrethrum has long been used in herbal remedies.
The garden contains several ornamental species of Anacyclus.

senses.definition: A wreath or band worn around the head, especially as a sign of honor or celebration.
examples: The statue shows the goddess wearing an anadem of leaves.
The victor received an anadem instead of a metal crown.

senses.definition: Wreaths or bands worn around the head, especially in a decorative or ceremonial way.
examples: The dancers wore anadems of fresh flowers.
In the painting, children play with ribbons and anadems.

senses.definition: Lack or absence of glands in a part of the body where they are normally present.
examples: Anadenia of the skin can affect sweat production.
The condition is characterised by partial anadenia of the salivary glands.

senses.definition: A band around a group of vertebrae in certain animals, especially in some crustaceans or worms.
examples: The segments are joined by a flexible anadesm.
Under the microscope, the anadesm between the vertebra‑like plates is visible.

senses.definition: Describing a pulse wave that shows two small rises during its upward phase.
examples: An anadicrotic pulse may signal abnormal arterial elasticity.
The tracing shows an anadicrotic pattern in the radial artery.

senses.definition: The condition in which an artery shows an anadicrotic pulse wave with two small rises.
examples: Anadicrotism may appear in advanced arterial disease.
The study records the frequency of anadicrotism in elderly patients.

senses.definition: A congenital condition in which the body develops a pair of parts where usually there is only one.
examples: Anadidymus can occur in various organs during early development.
The rare case of anadidymus was recorded in medical literature.

senses.definition: Instances of using anadiplosis, where a word at the end of one clause is repeated at the beginning of the next.
examples: The speech uses several anadiploses to build emotional force.
Writers employ anadiploses to guide the reader through connected ideas.

senses.definition: A rhetorical device in which a word or phrase at the end of one sentence or clause is repeated at the beginning of the next one to create emphasis or a smooth link.
examples: Anadiplosis can give a speech a strong sense of flow and logic.
In the line "Fear leads to anger; anger leads to hate," the repetition of "anger" is anadiplosis.

senses.definition: Excessive thirst, often as a symptom of disease.
examples: Anadipsia can be an early sign of diabetes.
The patient complained of constant anadipsia and fatigue.

senses.definition: Experiencing or relating to extreme or constant thirst.
examples: Anadipsic patients often drink large amounts of water.
The disorder can make a person anadipsic even in cool weather.

senses.definition: Short form used for an anadromous fish that migrates from the sea into fresh water to breed.
examples: The river supports several anadroms during the spawning season.
Overfishing has reduced the number of anadroms returning each year.

senses.definition: Describing fish that live in the sea but swim up rivers into fresh water to breed.
examples: Salmon are a classic example of anadromous species.
Many anadromous fish are threatened by dams and pollution.

senses.definition: A title given to Aphrodite as she rises from the sea, often used for artworks showing a goddess emerging from the water.
examples: The painting is an Anadyomene, showing the goddess stepping from the waves.
Classical artists often returned to the Anadyomene theme.

senses.definition: A condition with an unusually low amount of blood in part of the body.
examples: Anaematosis of the tissues can delay healing.
Prolonged pressure may lead to local anaematosis.

senses.definition: A medical condition in which the blood does not have enough healthy red cells, making a person feel weak and tired.
examples: Iron deficiency is a common cause of anaemia in young women.
The doctor ordered tests to check for anaemia.

senses.definition: Different kinds or cases of anaemia.
examples: Doctors distinguish between nutritional anaemias and genetic anaemias.
The study compared various anaemias in children under five.

senses.definition: Having anaemia, often looking pale and feeling weak.
examples: She felt too anaemic to climb the stairs.
The doctor said he was slightly anaemic and needed more iron.

senses.definition: Weak, lacking energy, force, or interest.
examples: The team’s anaemic performance disappointed their fans.
Sales growth has been anaemic for the past year.

senses.definition: In a way that shows anaemia, with weakness or paleness.
examples: She moved anaemically across the room.
He smiled anaemically, too tired to say much.

senses.definition: Difference in the way a substance conducts or absorbs something when measured in different directions.
examples: The crystal displays marked anaemotropy under stress.
Anaemotropy can affect how materials respond to magnetic fields.

senses.definition: Removing or taking something away, especially referring to surgical or medical removal.
examples: Anaeretic treatment was considered necessary to remove the infected tissue.
The surgeon chose an anaeretic approach to prevent further spread.

senses.definition: Growth or activity of organisms in the absence of oxygen.
examples: Anaerobation occurs in deep layers of the soil.
The tank is sealed to allow anaerobation of the bacteria.

senses.definition: A living organism, especially a bacterium, that can live without oxygen.
examples: Clostridium is a well‑known anaerobe.
Anaerobes often thrive in deep wounds.

senses.definition: Organisms, especially bacteria, that live or can live without oxygen.
examples: Anaerobes play an important role in breaking down organic matter in wetlands.
Some anaerobes cause serious infections in deep tissues.

senses.definition: Life processes or conditions that occur without oxygen.
examples: The lower layers of the lake are characterised by anaerobia.
Anaerobia in the soil affects nutrient cycling.

senses.definition: An organism that lives without oxygen; an anaerobe.
examples: The sample contained both aerobians and anaerobians.
Each anaerobian was identified by genetic analysis.

senses.definition: Needing little or no oxygen, or happening where there is no oxygen.
examples: Some bacteria grow best in anaerobic conditions.
Anaerobic exercise makes the muscles work hard in a short burst.

senses.definition: In a way that does not use or require oxygen.
examples: The waste is broken down anaerobically in large tanks.
During a sprint, muscles partly work anaerobically.

senses.definition: Organisms that live without oxygen; another form of the word anaerobes.
examples: The sample was rich in anaerobies due to the lack of oxygen.
Anaerobies can spoil food stored in sealed containers.
